metadata.title: Media and the Meanings of Land: A South Korean Case Study
metadata.journal: The American Journal of Economics and Sociology

abstract: How land and property are treated has changed through history: from commons to commodities and financial assets. Nevertheless, studies on how the media contribute to this process are rare. Based on Anne Haila's concept of "property mind" and the idea of "the culture of property," this article analyzes how the media have framed the meanings of land and reflected on the treatment of land. We analyzed editorials on land, housing, and properties from seven South Korean newspapers over a 30-year period using a structural topic model (STM). The results demonstrate that the media have played a vital role in contributing to the promotion of the private land tenure system and "the property mind" in South Korea, particularly since the late 1990s. Despite the recent trends that have intensified around market-oriented topics, we also found that liberal and conservative newspapers can be quite different in how they shape "the culture of property."

paragraphs: Land has multiple meanings: it is nature, a place in which one was born or came from, a resource contributing to livelihood, a shared space, a common, private property, a commodity, and an object of investment and speculation. These diverse meanings of land have shifted over time. In a capitalist society, land has been treated as a commodity and a financial asset, justified by the idea of property 382

The American Journal of Economics and Sociology rights (Haila 2016). In this article, we look at the meanings of land and the ways in which land is treated. We have not followed conventional ways of studying land either in terms of financial speculation or in terms of the role of states and municipalities in land management (Bae and Sellers 2007;Park 2008;Park and Han 2018). Instead, we critically analyze land through the lens of the media. In particular, we have investigated how newspaper editorials have portrayed and framed land, specifically how they treat the various meanings of land.

Our case study focuses on South Korea (henceforth, Korea) for the following three reasons. First, Korea has a long history of land reform, but there has been a paucity of land studies in the Korean context published in international journals. Second, it is known that Korean media have been powerful in mobilizing society (Kern and Nam 2009;Oh and Lee 2014;Shim 2002). However, there have been only a few studies on the role of the media in relation to land and property issues. Third, Korean society has experienced a surge in the real estate market since the 1970s (La Grange and Jung 2004;Park 2008). This occurred a decade before the global commodification of land driven by foreign investment in the international real estate market (Sassen 2014). Therefore, Seoul has become widely known as a principal hub of urban land development in East Asia (Shin 2011;Shin and Kim 2016). Previous studies of land development and the treatment of land have highlighted Singapore and Hong Kong (Chiu et al. 2018;Haila 2016). We aim to add Korea as a contribution to empirical research on land issues in East Asia.

Throughout history, the forms of land tenure have been divided into state forms and private forms (enclosed, privatized, and commodified). There are other viable forms of land tenure, including common ownership and customary land tenure (Cobb 2016;Obeng-Odoom 2016). In this article, our aim is neither to criticize nor to praise any particular form of land tenure. Based on our empirical data, the aim is rather to investigate the influential role of newspapers in framing and reflecting the conceptualization of land, which has varied over time according to the political, social, economic, and historical conditions that have shaped land (and housing) development. "Framing" refers to making one or more aspects of reality salient while obscuring others in a communication text; "reflecting" means to express and highlight an issue in a text of communication that illustrates the boundaries of discourse Media and the Meanings of Land embedded in the text (Entman 1993). Haila (2017: 500) questions how "the culture of property" is constructed and what motivates people to favor private property or to adopt "the property mind," which is an "appetite for property [that makes] them watchers of the property market." She argues that the culture of property differs across cities and countries depending on the local context. In this article, we have focused on whether and how the newspapers under study have contributed to a particular framing of land and created a particular culture of property.

We began with a review of studies on the Korean media (Chung 2004;Kang et al. 2013;Kim et al. 2013;Sa 2009a). We assumed that newspapers have had a long-running influential role in framing and reflecting views about social and economic issues in accordance with changing political climates and socioeconomic conditions. Therefore, the research questions addressed in this article are threefold.

1. How have Korean newspapers, through their editorials, framed and reflected the meanings of land and the types of land tenure during the 30-year period from 1990 to 2019? 2. What could be the political, economic, or social backdrop of these newspapers' editorial texts that discussed land issues? 3. Have liberal and conservative newspapers expressed different views on land issues?

To answer these research questions, we analyzed the text of newspaper editorials. We collected text data and recorded the information concerning the issue dates and names of newspapers. In all, we retrieved 167,349 editorial articles from seven newspapers by using an online database called BigKinds. These seven newspapers are Kyunghyang, Kukmin, Munhwa, Hankyoreh, Hankook, Segye, and Seoul. We categorized the partisanship of Kyunghyang and Hankyoreh as being liberal and Munwha and Kookmin as being conservative based on previous Korean media studies (Chung 2004;Kim et al. 2013;Lee and Kim 2006;Sa 2009b). Restricted access made it impossible for us to include core conservative newspapers, including Chosun, Donga, and Joongang.

The period covered the three decades from January 1990 to December 2019 for which we found data. Also, since 1988, Korea has The American Journal of Economics and Sociology been governed by civilian governments, and the media have enjoyed more freedom. More importantly, Korea had its first set of public land acts adopted in 1989. Hence, we consider 1990 as a turning point in introducing the concept of public land to citizens, and there is a need to analyze the role of the media in framing and reflecting this new concept.

We used a structural topic model (STM) to analyze the contents of the editorials. STM is an automated text analysis method based on word co-occurrences, allowing researchers to infer latent topics (sets of words with high co-occurrence probability) within a large collection of editorials. A primary advantage of STM is that it can be used to assess the effects of document-level covariates, including newspapers' partisanship and time, on topic prevalence, a term that refers to how frequently a topic is discussed in an editorial (Roberts et al. 2014).

Based on these data and methods of data collection and analysis, we argue that the media, through their newspaper editorials, have played a key role in contributing to the promotion of the private land tenure system and in stimulating "the property mind" in South Korea, particularly since the late 1990s. This article is organized into four sections. In the first section, we review earlier studies on related issues. In particular, we discuss the theoretical background and point to the political, economic, or social conditions that could be crucial elements in shaping the meanings of land over the period of 30 years. In the second section, we explain our data and the structural topic model (STM) as an analytical method. We then report quantitative model outputs and discuss their political, economic, and social backdrops in the third section. We end this article with conclusions in the fourth section.

sections.header_raw: The Meanings of Land and Property Rights
sections.header_norm: the meanings of land and property rights
paragraphs: Land ownership is a social issue and the forms of land ownership have been transformed throughout history (Christophers 2018;Haila 2016). Land has been differently treated as "being common, then [changed to being] enclosed, commodified, privatized and securitized" (Haila 2016: 27). The meaning of land has thus changed over time. As part of Media and the Meanings of Land nature, land was held in common and used by farmers and indigenous people with the intention of sustainable use without exploitation. The land users were members of the community (Haila 2016). Later, in the feudal system, land management reflected the relationship between landlords and tenants. "Property was a right and a relationship" (Haila 2016: 29), but the practices differed from society to society. For example, in the British feudal system, commons still existed in some areas as farmers and day laborers were granted common rights; they could benefit from the products of the commons by gathering firewood and by allowing animals, such as cows and pigs, to forage. After the arrival of capitalism, land was commodified through parceling and enclosures. "Enclosure and parceling changed the nature of land from a source of livelihood to a commodity, an object to be bought and sold"; under this capitalist regime, land market development emerged (Haila 2016: 30-31). The development modes under this regime include uncontrolled development of suburbs and the proliferation of gated communities (Haila 2016: 28;Boonjubun 2019). Karl Polanyi ([1944] 2001), a prominent political economist, was concerned about the idea of land being treated as a commodity. To him, land is a "fictitious commodity." He argues that, if land is bought and sold in the market, social and ecological impacts will occur, such as a decline in community ties and degradation of the environment. Henry George ([1883] 1981), in Social Problems, pointed out the centrality of the private appropriation of socially created land rent to socio-ecological problems. In the colonization era, the idea of enclosure was brought along with the European colonizers and introduced by them to the colonies outside Europe. In recent decades, land has been treated as a financial asset (Haila 2016).

Based on the discussion of the meanings of land, land tenure is usually divided into two types, namely: private and public ownership. Georgist political economists identify a third type: the commons (Haila 2011;Obeng-Odoom 2021). John Locke argued that when mixing labor into land, land possession is justified (Rose 1994). Locke's "labor theory of property" has been used to justify enclosure and private ownership. According to Haila's (2016: 42) analysis, Locke's notion of ownership is manifest in popular novels such as Daniel Defoe's Robinson Crusoe, "a story of possessive individualism." When

The American Journal of Economics and Sociology land is privately owned, the owner enjoys an exclusive right to the produce of that land, which includes rent and profit. Land owned by individuals, private firms, and even cities and authorities can now be bought, sold, rented out, and speculated upon.

Land tenure shapes how property rights to land are allocated. This property rights system has become the norm. Property rights theorists see property rights as a driving force to economic prosperity and a way to minimize transaction costs (Alchian 1961;Demsetz 1967;Yoo and Harris 2016;Yoo and Steckel 2010). Property rights are believed to be natural, which includes rights of full ownership or a bundle of rights. The "Coase Theorem" proposes that property rights increase the efficiency of markets by reducing transaction costs (Coase [1991(Coase [ ] 2008)). Obeng-Odoom (2020: 66) summarizes the practical implications of the theorem:

[It is] the idea that well-defined property rights reduce transaction costs to little or nothing and facilitate capitalist property transactions, which, in turn, enable economically lagging areas (such as African countries) to catch up with more prosperous areas (primarily in the West).

In the Mystery of Capital (2000), de Soto argues that formalizing and securing private property rights could alleviate poverty and bring about economic prosperity. Other scholars agree with de Soto that providing tenure security through a land titling program could secure the rights to property, increase residential investment in urban slums, and help reduce poverty (Field 2005;Yoo 2017). Obeng-Odoom (2020) examines this question also.

This belief in private property has been embedded in the "property lobby," which refers to the ways developers and landowners lobby policymakers to adopt policies that benefit their land development projects and real estate businesses (Haila 2016). The notion of private property has established what Haila (2017: 500) calls the "property mind" and "culture of property," which serve as catalysts that motivate people to buy, lease, sell, and invest as a way of profit-making in real estate. The belief in this property rights system also has been promoted by international development organizations such as the World Bank and the Asian Development Bank (ADB), which have promoted Media and the Meanings of Land land development policies and land use practices that replace agricultural commons with private land ownership by large corporations (Obeng-Odoom 2018, 2020).

In analyzing how land is treated, Haila (2016) focuses on the institutional history of land and housing development processes. Card et al. (2020) reflect on Haila's (2016: 64-69) intellectual strategy: instead of taking the approach of either radical political economy (land nationalization) or liberal economic theory (private land ownership), the state should take a strong role by confiscating land rent to achieve an equal distribution of land. This is in line with Henry George ([1879] 1981), who sought pragmatic solutions (taxing land rent) instead of ideological disputes. However, Haila's (2016) case study was Singapore, where the city-state has implemented a hybrid model by combining public and private ownership. Land questions are different in other cities (Haila 2016: xxii). For this article, we also took into consideration the Korean institutional history of land development processes that have influenced how newspaper editorials have treated land issues.

The land users were members of the community (Haila 2016). Later, in the feudal system, land management reflected the relationship between landlords and tenants. "Property was a right and a relationship" (Haila 2016: 29), but the practices differed from society to society. For example, in the British feudal system, commons still existed in some areas as farmers and day laborers were granted common rights; they could benefit from the products of the commons by gathering firewood and by allowing animals, such as cows and pigs, to forage. After the arrival of capitalism, land was commodified through parceling and enclosures. "Enclosure and parceling changed the nature of land from a source of livelihood to a commodity, an object to be bought and sold"; under this capitalist regime, land market development emerged (Haila 2016: 30-31). The development modes under this regime include uncontrolled development of suburbs and the proliferation of gated communities (Haila 2016: 28;Boonjubun 2019). Karl Polanyi ([1944] 2001), a prominent political economist, was concerned about the idea of land being treated as a commodity. To him, land is a "fictitious commodity." He argues that, if land is bought and sold in the market, social and ecological impacts will occur, such as a decline in community ties and degradation of the environment. Henry George ([1883] 1981), in Social Problems, pointed out the centrality of the private appropriation of socially created land rent to socio-ecological problems. In the colonization era, the idea of enclosure was brought along with the European colonizers and introduced by them to the colonies outside Europe. In recent decades, land has been treated as a financial asset (Haila 2016).

Based on the discussion of the meanings of land, land tenure is usually divided into two types, namely: private and public ownership. Georgist political economists identify a third type: the commons (Haila 2011;Obeng-Odoom 2021). John Locke argued that when mixing labor into land, land possession is justified (Rose 1994). Locke's "labor theory of property" has been used to justify enclosure and private ownership. According to Haila's (2016: 42) analysis, Locke's notion of ownership is manifest in popular novels such as Daniel Defoe's Robinson Crusoe, "a story of possessive individualism." When

The American Journal of Economics and Sociology land is privately owned, the owner enjoys an exclusive right to the produce of that land, which includes rent and profit. Land owned by individuals, private firms, and even cities and authorities can now be bought, sold, rented out, and speculated upon.

Land tenure shapes how property rights to land are allocated. This property rights system has become the norm. Property rights theorists see property rights as a driving force to economic prosperity and a way to minimize transaction costs (Alchian 1961;Demsetz 1967;Yoo and Harris 2016;Yoo and Steckel 2010). Property rights are believed to be natural, which includes rights of full ownership or a bundle of rights. The "Coase Theorem" proposes that property rights increase the efficiency of markets by reducing transaction costs (Coase [1991(Coase [ ] 2008)). Obeng-Odoom (2020: 66) summarizes the practical implications of the theorem:

[It is] the idea that well-defined property rights reduce transaction costs to little or nothing and facilitate capitalist property transactions, which, in turn, enable economically lagging areas (such as African countries) to catch up with more prosperous areas (primarily in the West).

In the Mystery of Capital (2000), de Soto argues that formalizing and securing private property rights could alleviate poverty and bring about economic prosperity. Other scholars agree with de Soto that providing tenure security through a land titling program could secure the rights to property, increase residential investment in urban slums, and help reduce poverty (Field 2005;Yoo 2017). Obeng-Odoom (2020) examines this question also.

This belief in private property has been embedded in the "property lobby," which refers to the ways developers and landowners lobby policymakers to adopt policies that benefit their land development projects and real estate businesses (Haila 2016). The notion of private property has established what Haila (2017: 500) calls the "property mind" and "culture of property," which serve as catalysts that motivate people to buy, lease, sell, and invest as a way of profit-making in real estate. The belief in this property rights system also has been promoted by international development organizations such as the World Bank and the Asian Development Bank (ADB), which have promoted Media and the Meanings of Land land development policies and land use practices that replace agricultural commons with private land ownership by large corporations (Obeng-Odoom 2018, 2020).

In analyzing how land is treated, Haila (2016) focuses on the institutional history of land and housing development processes. Card et al. (2020) reflect on Haila's (2016: 64-69) intellectual strategy: instead of taking the approach of either radical political economy (land nationalization) or liberal economic theory (private land ownership), the state should take a strong role by confiscating land rent to achieve an equal distribution of land. This is in line with Henry George ([1879] 1981), who sought pragmatic solutions (taxing land rent) instead of ideological disputes. However, Haila's (2016) case study was Singapore, where the city-state has implemented a hybrid model by combining public and private ownership. Land questions are different in other cities (Haila 2016: xxii). For this article, we also took into consideration the Korean institutional history of land development processes that have influenced how newspaper editorials have treated land issues.

sections.header_raw: Property Rights in Korea
sections.header_norm: property rights in korea
paragraphs: The idea of private property tenure was introduced to Asia by European colonizers (Haila 2016). Later, individual property rights were promoted in Southeast Asia through privatization and land titling programs supported by the World Bank and other development aid organizations, especially in the 1980s during the time of structural adjustment (Boonjubun et al. 2021;Obeng-Odoom 2018). Since then, the private property rights system has become a dominating system, replacing customary and communal land (Mitchell et al. 2008;Wittayapak and Baird 2018).

In Korea, in the Chosun Dynasty (1392-1897) the state hypothetically owned the land (La Grange and Jung 2004). Nevertheless, the state allocated the use and inheritable rights to the gentry class by allowing them to collect rents from farmers during the late Chosun period. When Japan colonized Korea in 1910, the colonial government conducted cadastral surveys and issued certificates of ownership to those who already held the use rights; it also sold unoccupied lands to the Japanese or Japanese collaborators. This evolving new land

The American Journal of Economics and Sociology system laid the foundation of private property ownership in Korea (La Grange and Jung 2004). Arguably, Japanese colonizers brought the private land tenure system to Korea (Yoo and Steckel 2010). Secure property rights were promoted to solve public finance problems and to contribute to economic growth (Yoo and Steckel 2010). As a legacy of Japanese colonization, despite many attempts to reform the country's land system throughout subsequent decades, the tenets of private ownership have remained intact in Korea (La Grange and Jung 2004).

After Japanese colonization, Korea remained a poor agrarian country. The property market was not vitalized until the authoritarian military regime implemented government-led and export-oriented economic policies in the 1970s. The state concentrated the use of limited national resources in developing a few strategic regions and cities, including the Seoul metropolitan area, which resulted in longstanding uneven land development among regions and cities (Park 2008). While the state focused on land as an object for city development, such as the construction of industrial infrastructure, it relied heavily on a few large corporations and private investors who played a vital role in land and housing development. Hence, this period saw the beginning of private-sector involvement in real estate markets (La Grange and Jung 2004;Park 1998Park , 2008;;Shin and Kim 2016).

Even though public land acts were introduced in 1989, the amount of land owned by private individuals and corporations has increased. La Grange and Jung (2004: 578) argue that in the 1990s "land and housing development [became] more market-oriented, a process greatly facilitated by the liberalization of access to credit for housing." After the 1997 Asian Financial Crisis, land and housing prices in Korea fell steeply. However, in 2002, land and housing price inflation became a public concern, pressuring the Roh Moo-Hyun administration to introduce a range of measures in subsequent years that included "expanding supply, restraining speculation through taxation, special regulation in overheated market zones and the adaptation of homeownership schemes" (Ronald andJin 2010: 2373). In 2005, the administration reinforced the real estate policy by complementing the existing measures and enacting the comprehensive real estate tax due to the resurgence of speculation. This real estate tax was drawn up in 2003 and Media and the Meanings of Land implemented in 2005 with an aim "to restrain speculation and realign inconsistent local tax systems" (Ronald andJin 2010: 2381). 1 Despite these comprehensive measures, Korean governments have had difficulty controlling speculation (Kim and Lee 2017). In the present day, most of the land area is owned by private entities in Korea, especially a few financially or politically powerful entities. According to statistical data on land ownership reported by the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, and Transport (2018), the proportion of private land (owned by individuals, associations, corporations, and non-corporations) is 66.7 percent across the country and 53.2 percent in Seoul. Within the total amount of land owned by individuals, only 1 percent of the population (0.5 million people) occupies 53.7 percent of national lands and 13.4 percent of the Seoul area, which accounts for 33.8 percent of the market value of land nationally and 46.2 percent of the value in Seoul. In this article, we seek to examine whether the Korean newspaper editorials have played a vital role in promoting the idea of private property.

sections.paper_id: Shin and Boonjubun - 2021 - Media and the Meanings of Land A South Korean Case Study
sections.header_raw: A Brief History of Korean Media
sections.header_norm: a brief history of korean media
paragraphs: In this section, we outline the historical trajectory of media in social change and vice versa (Sa 2009a;Park et al. 2000;Shim 2002). During the authoritarian regime, Korean media formed a close relationship with the state (kwonunyuchak) and acted as a conduit for the state's political propaganda, which was used in order to control citizens. During the period of Japanese rule , the colonial government imposed restrictions on Korean media. Seoul-based newspapers were closed down except for a few "underground" and "mouthpiece" newspapers (Sa 2009a).

The media's role changed during the period of American military rule (1945)(1946)(1947)(1948)(1949)(1950)(1951)(1952)(1953)(1954)(1955)(1956)(1957)(1958)(1959)(1960)(1961) because Korean media began to work under a new form of freedom. In 1945, the country witnessed three movements: citizens, an anti-Japanese group, and a pro-Japanese group. The members of the last group were afraid of losing the property they accumulated during Japanese rule (Sa 2009a). After the liberation of Korea, the pro-Japanese group became powerful and transformed itself into a pro-American group. Under the military regimes , restrictions were applied to the press again, through the Constitution The American Journal of Economics and Sociology of the 3 rd Republic (1963), but the media were permitted to publish on nonpolitical issues (Sa 2009a). From the 3 rd to 5 th Republics, the Korean media were controlled by means of censorship and manipulation, as well as other "indirect" means (Park et al. 2000: 102). This was to maintain the power of the military governments.

From 1988 to the present, Korea has been administered by civilian governments, and freedom of the press has become visible. Nonetheless, Korean media have faced a new challenge: the power of global capital in the era of multimedia and competition among a large number of domestic and foreign companies (Sa 2009a;Shim 2002). Since the 1990s, large Korean companies have invested in almost every subsector of the media, including film, broadcasting, and newspapers, in order to establish media conglomerates (Shim 2002). As a result, most newspapers, including the seven newspapers studied for this article, became privately owned. The media revolution in the early 2000s strengthened the power of media (including K-pop, the Korean popular music industry), but this made their businesses highly dependent on advertising revenue that could jeopardize their freedom (Oh and Lee 2014;Sa 2009b;Shim 2002).

Against the backdrop of media liberalization and side effects (Shim 2002), many studies examine media bias in Korean newspapers, both quantitatively and qualitatively (Chung 2004;Kim et al. 2013;Lee and Kim 2006;Sa 2009aSa , 2009b)). Media bias refers to consistent patterns in the choice of news (gatekeeping bias), news content (coverage bias), and opinions on an issue (statement bias) that shape readers' interpretations of perceived reality (Eisinger et al. 2007;Entman 2007). Kim et al. (2013) conducted a quantitative content analysis of eight Korean newspapers' editorials and their political views and found that Hankyoreh and Kyunghyang tend to emphasize democracy, equality, and justice, and that Munwha stresses accountability, being lawabiding, and growth. Several studies view the former as liberals and the latter as conservative in the Korean context (Chung 2004;Kim et al. 2013;Lee and Kim 2006;Sa 2009b).

We neither judge media bias as inherently good or bad nor assume that a particular newspaper demonstrates an entirely liberal or conservative bias. Rather, our focus was to identify diverging voices in Media and the Meanings of Land editorials. Therefore, we have taken these previous studies to reveal tendencies. Based on previous studies, we assumed that Hankyoreh and Kyunghyang would be liberal-leaning, while Munwha could take a conservative standpoint when discussing land issues, albeit without a presumption of their contents. We also considered Kukmin to be conservative. Kukmin was founded in 1988 by a Christian-affiliated foundation representing Christianity and the Korean church community (Chung 2004;Sa 2009b). Kukmin may take a conservative position on specific issues, such as the family headship system (Lee and Kim 2006) and genetic research (Chung 2004). We assumed that Kukmin would take a conservative position when framing land topics, since the Korean church has been actively involved in the real estate market to extend its influence and to build churches and other religious buildings in urban areas (Han 1997;Lee 2014).

sections.header_raw: Data and Method
sections.header_norm: data and method
paragraphs: In the previous section, we discussed the historical changes of the land ownership system in Korea after the authoritarian regime. We note that different people, authorities, organizations, and groups (both public and private) perceive different meanings of land in different contexts. While Haila (2016) focused her work on Singapore and its land administration, we have focused on Korea and the role of the Korean media in framing and reflecting on the meanings of land. Media studies have shown that newspapers shape public opinion and that partisanship influences this role. We assumed that partisanship would be a factor that differentiates each newspaper's view of land issues. In this section, we discuss the data collection and methods.

Drawing on newspaper editorials, we investigated how Korean newspapers have framed and reflected land topics for the last 30 years. A newspaper editorial is an article that conveys the opinion of the senior editors or external experts on topics selected by the editorial desk. Editorials do not aim merely to deliver information on a situation or an incident but also to construct reasonable arguments to influence the public and decision-makers. Therefore, analyzing the contents of editorials can provide empirical evidence of media orientation.

sections.header_raw: The American Journal of Economics and Sociology Data Collection and Preprocessing
sections.header_norm: the american journal of economics and sociology data collection and preprocessing
paragraphs: We collected editorials in seven national newspapers-Kyunghyang, Kukmin, Munhwa, Hankyoreh, Hankook, Segye, andSeoul-published between 1990 and2019. Korean newspapers normally publish two editorials per day. These editorials were retrieved from BigKinds, which is a large online database managed by the Korea Press Foundation, a quasi-autonomous nongovernmental organization. BigKinds contains an extensive collection of digitized articles from national and local newspapers and shares the data with the public via a state-of-theart online platform. 2 Of the 10 national newspapers in Korea, we collected data from the above seven newspapers because Chosun, Donga, and Joongang have not made their data between 1990 and 2018 accessible to the public.

The 177,020 editorials retrieved from the seven newspapers were sorted and grouped by title, text, issue date, newspaper, and keyword. Due to copyright issues, BigKinds separately provides text data (200 words) and keywords (all nouns and noun phrases in the full content) (Park 2016;Park et al. 2017). We employed editorial text and keywords in analyzing the content. We considered the issue date (time) and newspaper's name (partisanship) as factors affecting the published content. Since BigKinds collects data from online newspapers, the database can contain duplicate articles carried in sister newspapers and magazines (Park 2016). The data were preprocessed in Python in order to remove these duplicate articles, stop words, punctuation, and numbers. The preprocess yielded 167,349 editorials in total, as shown in Table 1. We identified Kyunghyang and Hankyoreh as "Liberals" and Kukmin and Munhwa as "Conservatives," and the other three newspapers as "Moderates."

sections.header_raw: Structural Topic Model (STM)
sections.header_norm: structural topic model stm
paragraphs: Content analysis is a method used to describe content and draw inferences about its meaning (Riffe et al. 2005). We analyzed digitized text data. Traditionally, researchers read documents and manually extracted information based on predefined coding frameworks. This method is suitable if the collection of documents is relatively small and well-defined. However, the number of editorials we analyzed Media and the Meanings of Land made manual coding inappropriate. Thus, we used a topic model for the analysis of text data.

A topic model is a type of statistical model designed for automatically finding hidden topical patterns within a collection of documents (Barde and Bainwad 2017). Among several topic modeling techniques, the Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), developed by Blei and colleagues, is widely used (Blei 2012;Blei et al. 2003). However, LDA and other earlier models do not offer a well-established framework to feed observable metadata information into the model (Roberts et al. 2016). That is, news editorials are assumed to be generated by the same underlying process, such as being written by the same author. More specifically, editorials are assumed to fall within a globally shared Dirichlet distribution with specified parameters (Blei 2012). This assumption lacks applicability for analyzing news editorials since their contents might be influenced by time and partisanship. Instead, we chose the structural topic model (STM) which was purposely developed to overcome the limitations of LDA (Roberts et al. 2013(Roberts et al. , 2014(Roberts et al. , 2016)).

paragraphs: The American Journal of Economics and Sociology words without order that is likely to co-occur in the same context. For instance, a "land" topic would give a higher probability to words such as house and property; and a "legislation" topic would give a higher probability to words such as law and property. Each document can include both land and legislation topics in different proportions. It is also possible that the same word, for instance, the word property, can be included in both topics. This assumption enables the STM to randomly generate documents based on probabilistic procedures and then use the corpus data to infer hidden topical information (Roberts et al. 2019: 2).

Figure 1 displays the probabilistic generation process of STM (Roberts et al. 2016). Gray and white nodes are observed and hidden variables, respectively, labeled according to specific roles in the process. Consider a collection of documents D, indexed by d ∈ {1⋯D}, each containing empty N d positions, each of which will be filled with terms drawn from a corpus-level vocabulary V , the elements of which are indexed by v ∈ {1⋯V }. Instances of terms that fill these positions are called words, denoted as w. In Korean, two words, such as "land Media and the Meanings of Land use," or three words, such as "land transfer tax," are often combined to become a morpheme, called an n-gram. We treat unigrams, bigrams, and trigrams as w. The model assumes that the researcher has specified the number of topics K, indexed by k ∈ {1⋯K}. In addition to the observed words w d,n , there are two matrices of topic prevalence covariates X and topic content covariates Y . These are the basic settings of the STM (Roberts et al. 2016).

The model has three components, as shown in Figure 1: topic prevalence parameters, which control how much a document is associated with topics (document-topic proportions d ); topic content parameters, which control how words in a topic vary (topic-word distributions d,k,v ); and the core language model that combines these parameters to generate words in each document (Roberts et al. 2016). The core language model of the STM and LDA is identical (Roberts et al. 2016). The model follows a two-step process (Blei 2012: 78):
Step 1: Randomly choose a distribution over topics d .

Step 2: For each empty position N d in the document d, randomly choose a topic z d,n from the distribution over topics d in step 1. Randomly choose a word w n from the corresponding distribution over the vocabulary d,k,v .

The unique feature of STM is that topic prevalence parameters d and topic content parameters d,k,v are no longer influenced by globally shared prior distributions, as in the LDA, but by their own prior distribution of topics, defined by document-level covariates (Roberts et al. 2016). For a model with K topics, document topic proportions d are influenced by covariates X through a standard linear model, with covariates drawn from the logistic normal distribution (Roberts et al. 2016). It allows the incorporation of extra information embedded in metadata to estimate the effects of document-level covariates on land topics in the form of generalized linear models. The effects of topic prevalence covariates using newspaper's names (n = 7) and issue dates (ranging from 1990 to 2019) on document topic proportions d were considered for this article. We used an STM package (Roberts et al. 2019) in the R environment, which provides tools to estimate the above parameters by maximizing the posterior likelihood
The American Journal of Economics and Sociology of the observed data (w d,n and X) that were generated by the datagenerating process.

Overall, the primary goal of utilizing the STM for this article was to infer hidden topic structures in the document collection by using observed texts and associated metadata. Based on the model outputs, researchers read representative texts of each topic and interpreted the contents. Therefore, our analysis combined the results of the quantified topic models and our qualitative interpretation. Since Korean news editorials are written in Korean, most of the model outputs were in Korean. We translated the Korean words (especially the topics in editorials) into English by using the Naver Dictionary (https://en.dict. naver.com/), which is a popular online Korean-English dictionary in Korea, and we ensured the validity of the translated words by crosschecking these words in the English academic literature. Moreover, we considered a number of empirical studies that used STM as they provided a useful guide for us to systematically report our findings (Chandelier et al. 2018;Hu et al. 2019;Kim et al. 2020;Roberts et al. 2013Roberts et al. , 2014Roberts et al. , 2016)).

sections.header_norm: data analysis
paragraphs: Based on a collection of editorials we first used an STM model to identify land and property topics and to trace their proportional change over time. Next, we focused on editorials that mainly discussed land and property topics and then used another STM model to identify particular subtopics under land and property topics and to analyze time and partisanship on topic prevalence.

sections.header_raw: Twenty Prevalent Topics in News Editorials
sections.header_norm: twenty prevalent topics in news editorials
paragraphs: What have been the most frequently mentioned words in Korean news editorials for the last 30 years? Investigating the top 10 words offered us a good starting point for understanding how editorials have not only reported what happened, but also reflected the socioeconomic conditions and political situations in Korean society at the particular points in time that we studied. The top 10 words (based on the occurrence frequency in parentheses) include: government (246,176),president (162,743),North Korea (143,723), Media and the Meanings of Land people (131,542), USA (97,761), prosecution (79,227), national assembly (73,258), fact (73,251), politics (70,483), and circumstance (67,003). The result implies that the collected editorials were written for national newspapers, which primarily discussed the presidential system and diplomacy. It is also noticeable that editorials have frequently used the words "fact" and "circumstance," implying that the nature of editorials is aimed at making the author's point with a diagnosis of present problems.

Then, we fitted a structural topic model (STM) by specifying 20 topics. Since we collected decades of editorials, the model could contain hundreds or perhaps thousands of topics. Our aim was to identify land-related topics that are likely to contain keywords such as land (ttang, toji), property (budongsan, geonmul, bilding), and house (jip, jutaeg, apateu). We considered these keywords to be indicators of land topics and repeated the model execution by increasing topic number K ≥ 2 until we found identifiable and interpretable land topics. We found meaningful outputs in the 20-topic model displayed in Table 2. In the table, the third column lists the top keywords associated with each topic, and the second column reports the estimated topic proportions, which are the outputs of the model. We read the keywords and texts of corresponding editorials and named each topic in the first column, based on our interpretation.

The result shows that inter-Korean relations was a major topic of editorials during the period of investigation. It consisted of such keywords in Korean as "the Korean peninsula," "summit," "inter-Korean relationship," "denuclearization," and "the international community," which represent diplomatic efforts related to nuclear deterrence. We also found many editorials about sporadic provocations between the North and South, such as the tragic events of the Cheonan warship sinking and the Yeonpyeong Island bombing in 2010, that might have escalated into an all-out war (topic 15). The high proportion of topics about North Korea reflects the fact that the Korean War ended in a truce, not a peace treaty, leaving the Korean peninsula technically still at war. Gills (2005: xvi), in Korea Versus Korea: A Case of Contested Legitimacy, noted that "the Korean conflict is one of the most persistent and intense of its kind in modern international relations." Other major topics revolved around domestic politics. Editorials discussed diverse political issues within and between political parties (topics 2 and 6), the president (topic 3), and the judiciary (topic 5), a topic that encompasses legal, administrative, and judicial power. Editorials have made allegations of political corruption in these bodies (topic 7), which implies rampant corruption among politicians, albeit continuously unveiled by relatively certain freedom of speech (Sa 2009a). Editorials have also given significant coverage to the global economy and possible impacts on the domestic economy (topic 4), corporations (topic 8), and the labor market (topic 11). Lastly, we found the topic of land and property (topic 13), along with other social issues, including online debates (topic 10), environment (topic 16), education (topics 17 and 19), social welfare (topic 18), and social movements (topic 20).

In particular, the topic of land and property consisted of "real estate, apartment, Seoul metropolitan area, municipality, people, Sejong city, national government, greenbelt, and new town." As to "apartment," the STM results show that the word "apartment" appeared more often than "house." According to the Population and Housing Census (Statistics Korea 2018), apartments comprised 61.4 percent of housing units in 2018, compared to detached houses (22.4 percent) and multi-unit houses (12.1 percent). Gelézeau (2007) referred to the dominance of apartment complexes in Korea as "Apartment Republic" and traced its historical roots from housing policy in the 1970s. In response to the housing shortage in urban areas, the authoritarian regime attracted middle-class families into large-scale apartment complexes by compensating them via ever-increasing apartment prices. In this context, an apartment became a commodity and the symbol of wealth, serving as a means to spatially separate owners from the lower classes (Gelézeau 2007: 134). Owning an apartment also means owning land rights. paragraphs: Korean context, apartment price consists of building price and land price. We consider apartment (housing) problems to be highly associated with land problems in the Korean context.

We outlined 20 major topics from the editorials published for the period of 30 years and situated land topics in the broader Korean context. The result shows that news editorials have treated land issues as one of the more critical social agendas. We also estimated the longitudinal change in the proportion of land topics based on the information of issue date. The data were initially formatted as "yearmonth-date" (1991-01-22) then converted into a "year" (1991) format considering the long period of investigation. We treated the year covariate (from 1990 to 2019) continuously and estimated a nonlinear relationship to examine how topic proportions vary as a function of year, as displayed in Figure 2 (Roberts et al. 2019). The line shows a long-term trend of land topics with 95 percent confidence intervals expressed as dotted lines. The result shows a cyclic fluctuation in land topics, with a low peak in 1994, followed by a high peak in 2005.

When the first cycle started in 1989, the rate of increase in land prices reached 30 percent (Shin 2009). The National Assembly aimed to shift the curve down and enacted three progressive acts called "three acts based on the public land concept" under the conservative Roh Tae-Woo administration-the Abnormal Capital Gains Tax Act, the Development Charge Act, and the Residential Land Ceiling Act. The first act was to tax idle land when the land price increased at a rate higher than that of the national average (on an accrual basis); the second act aimed at recapturing excessive development profits by levying fees on them; and the third act regulated residential land held for speculative purposes (Lee 1990: 121). The Korean Public Land Ownership Committee established for the legislation states:

It is desirable to promote the land market by preventing market failures through land use regulations, land tax systems, and limited land expropriation based on the private land ownership system. (Public Land Ownership Committee 1989: 23, cited in Lee 2007: 7) The three acts were designed to manage private land tenure, which was already predominant in Korea before the 1990s (La Grange and

paragraphs: declared partially unconstitutional by the Supreme Court in 2008. It also faced public criticism for its failure to alleviate land speculation, according to "An Unconstitutional Petition, including Article 5 of the Comprehensive Real Estate Holding Tax Act" (Lim 2009). Nevertheless, it laid a foundation for the Korean land tax system through several revisions.

After 2005, as Figure 2 shows, the proportion of land topics gradually decreased for 12 consecutive years. In 2017, the year of the impeachment of President Park Geun-Hye, the proportion of the topic reached its nadir at 3 percent. However, an increase in 2018 signaled the beginning of the fourth cycle, which was coupled with problems from wealth inequality; this urged the liberal Moon Jae-In administration to consider reinforcing the policy on real estate speculation (Choi 2020;Lee and Kim 2020).

Overall, the longitudinal change in the proportion of land topics shows that the two land reforms on private and public land influenced newspapers to write about land topics. Land reform does not mean the abandonment of private ownership but revision of government intervention in the land market through regulation, taxation, and expropriation (Lee 1990). La Grange and Jung (2004: 557) claim that "the state has had a major impact on the processes by which land has become commodified, using extensive land expropriation and land use planning powers." The first two cycles started in the conservative regime, and the last two cycles started in the liberal regime. In the era of neoliberalism, the state did not withdraw but became more proactive in swinging the market-state pendulum (Kim and Lee 2017;La Grange and Jung 2004;Lee 1990).

paragraphs: As previously explained, the document-topic proportions capture how much each topic contributes to a document, represented by the expected proportion of documents on each topic. For instance, assume that 59 percent of article A consisted of a land topic, 27 percent of an economy topic, and minor proportions of 18 other topics. By checking which topic has the highest value, we inferred the dominant topic of each article. In this example, the model suggests that article A mainly discussed a land topic. We further processed the filtered articles by checking whether the word land (ttang, toji), property (budongsan, geonmul, bilding), or house (jip, jutaeg, apateu) appeared in the content at least once. The two-step process yielded 3,399 editorials.

The next crucial task was to decide the number of topics within the editorials. What is the optimal number of topics within a collection of 3,399 editorials? As suggested by Roberts et al. (2019) and Kim et al. (2020), we used four diagnostic methods that guide validation in selecting the number of topics for a model (Figure 3). First, exclusivity measures the extent to which high-probability terms in one topic are not overlapped with high-probability terms in other topics. For instance, if the word "land" has a high probability in both the land topic and the education topic, it is considered to be nonexclusive; thus, the model obtains a lower exclusivity score. Second, semantic coherence measures how high-probability terms of a topic tend to occur together across articles. For instance, if the word "land" and "property" co-occur in many documents, the topic model is considered to be coherent, and it thus obtains a higher score. Therefore, semantic coherence and exclusivity should be checked in tandem. Third, held-out likelihood measures "the probability of unseen held-out documents given some training documents" (Wallach et al. 2009(Wallach et al. : 1006)). Here, held-out documents refer to documents in the test set, not in the training set, holding for cross-validation. It represents how well the model represents the statistics of the held-out articles; thus, a better model will give rise to a higher held-out likelihood. Fourth, residual checks "for overdispersion of the variance of the multinomial within the data generating process of STM" (Roberts et al. 2019: 38). In short, a better model would be higher on exclusivity, semantic coherence, and heldout likelihood, but low on residuals.

paragraphs: Models in this range were then qualitatively evaluated by their interpretability, and a 20-topic model was finally chosen.
The 20 Major Topics About Land and Property

Table 3 presents the result of the most frequent 20-topic model in association with "land" and "property" topics. Following Chandelier et al. (2018), we screened the contents of the identified topics and chose representative texts based on interpretation. We classified the 20 topics into four categories: (1) development, (2) social problems and countermeasures, (3) land reform, and (4) uninterpretable topics.

First, six topics within development (category 1) generally discussed land and housing development led by the state. Park (1998Park ( , 2008) ) argued that there was a long-standing coalition between the state and Chaebol-large corporations run by families-that created a unique model of public-private partnership in land and housing development. Park (1998) explains that many political leaders, acting as individual landholders and speculators, saw development as a rent-seeking vehicle. The state drew up development plans that made room for private investment (Cho and Križnik 2016;Park 1998). We identified topics that discussed these various "plans" of reconstruction of residential buildings (topic 5), area redevelopment under the label "new town" (topic 4), and greenbelt development (topic 6), centering around the Seoul Metropolitan Area (topic 1). 3 We also found plans for making cities outside of the metropolitan area decorated with miscellaneous adjectives such as "innovative," "scientific," "enterprise" (topic 2), and administrative capital (topic 3).

Second, we found eight topics that raised diverse social problems and countermeasures (category 2). Topics of faulty construction (topic 7) and the environment (topic 9) discussed the side effects of sprawling developments. We also found topics that discussed the government's countermeasures addressing abnormal price upswings in the house-rental (topic 10) and the land-and-housing market (topic 14) caused by the prevailing real estate speculation (topic 12) that led to social conflicts between renters and landlords (topic 11). Editorials also highlighted many NIMBY cases in which the authorities and Media and the Meanings of Land residents have had a long period of confrontation over available sites for unwanted public facilities (topic 8). 4 Lastly, we also found topics that discussed possible collusive links between political and business circles (topic 13).

Third, we found three topics of land reform (category 3) aimed at eradicating land monopoly and speculation. The topics discussed political struggles over the concept of public land ownership (topic 17), the revision of the landholding tax (topic 15), and the land transfer tax (topic 16). Fourth, we categorized three topics as uninterpretable. These are the government sector (topic 18), the political sector (topic 19), and the civil sector (topic 20). Although these topics mainly discussed different political actors, we considered them as uninterpretable (category 4) because they are cross-cutting topics that do not belong to any single umbrella topic.

paragraphs: The analysis of the 20-topic model in the previous subsection reveals that land and property topics can be divided into two types: development and counter-development. A topic itself is a powerful tool to represent and to promote a particular version of reality; it frames how people interpret social phenomena and make judgments (Entman 1993;Vliegenthart and van Zoonen 2011). In the STM, topic prevalence captures how much each topic contributes to an editorial (Roberts et al. 2019). Examining prevalence is useful for analyzing content bias, which is defined as "consistent patterns in the framing of mediated communication that promote the influence of one side in conflict over the use of government power" (Entman 2007: 166). Based on previous studies that classified newspapers as liberal or conservative, we statistically examined the difference of topic prevalence in editorials of these two opposing political views. The null hypothesis is that there is no difference between the liberal and the conservative editorials in topic prevalence.

Figure 4 is a plot of the results. The dots in the center are the mean values of the differences, and the bars of each topic show the 90 percent confidence intervals for the difference. Therefore, if the bar crosses the dotted lines, it means a failure to reject the null hypothesis.

paragraphs: For instance, the topic "Seoul Metropolitan Area" shows the mean value of -0.012 with the 90 percent confidence interval from -0.024 to -0.0006, indicating that the estimated proportion of news editorials that talked about the topic is 1.2 percent lower in the liberal news editorials. Because the bar did not cross the vertical line, the difference has statistical significance. In other words, the partisanship of newspapers influenced the topic prevalence of "Seoul Metropolitan Area."

Among 17 topics (after excluding three topics in the uninterpretable category), seven showed a statistically significant partisan difference. Liberal news editorials discussed "redevelopment," "NIMBY," "environment," and "rent conflict" significantly more than their counterparts. Conservative news editorials discussed "Seoul Metropolitan Area," "reconstruction," and "faulty construction" significantly more than their counterparts. The other 10 topics ("planned cities," "administrative Media and the Meanings of Land capital," "greenbelt development," "house-rental price," "speculation," "corruption," "land and housing price," "land holding tax," "land transfer tax," and "public land concept") appear frequently in both liberal and conservative editorials with no significant difference, suggesting that partisanship may not be a primary factor in influencing overall land topics. However, as Figure 4 shows, we found that development and land reform are slightly more conservative-leaning, whereas the topics of social problems are more liberal-leaning. Based on the statistical differences in social problem topics ("NIMBY," "environment," and "rent conflict"), we could argue that liberal editorials tend to be more concerned about social problems than their counterparts.

In the category of development (Figure 5), the "greenbelt development" topic and the The American Journal of Economics and Sociology "Seoul Metropolitan Area" topic have steadily decreased in prevalence over the years, implying that many development projects have taken place already within the metropolitan area. In contrast, the topics of redevelopment and reconstruction have received increasing attention from editorials, implying the rapid deterioration of the inner-city area and subsequent social problems (Cho 2005;Shin 2019;Shin and Kim 2016). These trend lines show that land topics have shifted from development (Seoul Metropolitan Area and greenbelt development) to redevelopment (reconstruction and redevelopment) during the period of investigation.

Reconstruction and development projects usually contribute to an increase in exchange value, rather than use value (Choi 2009). In the category of social problems and countermeasures, we found a gradual rise in the topics "house-rental" and "landand-housing prices," as shown in Figure 7. The two topics illustrate an increase in attention in newspaper editorials to the price Media and the Meanings of Land of land and houses, either for lease or for sale. The "speculation" and the "rent conflict" topics, which concern overheating markets and reflect social problems, exhibited a continuous decrease. In the category of land system reforms, there has been an upward trend in the topics "landholding tax" and "land transfer tax," but the topic "public land concept" significantly decreased, as shown in Figure 6.

What is the implication of these longitudinal changes in topic prevalence? While there is a pattern of increase in topics related to house prices and taxes and a decline in topics related to speculation, rent conflict, and the public land concept, we argue that price-related topics regarding land and housing illustrate media interest in the financial aspects of real estate. It can be inferred from this that the media have contributed to a trend to depict land as a mere commodity.

A major shift took place during the period of our study from the public land concept to the idea of land as a commodity. We found that 1997 and 2005 were the crucial turning points for changes in the way land was understood in Korea. Those years were intersecting points in Figures 5, 6, and 7.

The first of those turning points, 1997, was the year of the Asian Financial Crisis. The average price of Korean land dropped by 13.6 percent between 1997and 1998(Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, and Transport 1998). Prior to that, the price of land in major cities had increased by a factor of 28.9 between 1974 and 1996; in Seoul, it rose 32.9 times ( Jung 1998: 136, cited in Shin 2009: 908). The unprecedented price slump in 1998 shocked Korean society. The Kim Dae-Jung administration started to fundamentally restructure its politico-economic policies along neoliberal lines as a condition for a bailout by the International Monetary Fund (IMF) (Lee 2010;Shin 2019). It attempted to boost the faltering national economy by bolstering the real estate market, which included relief from the land-transfer tax and abolition of two public land acts that had been adopted in 1989-the Development Charge Act and the Residential Land Ceiling Act (Choi 2002). It also opened Korea to foreign capital and established real estate financial security systems, including asset backed securities (ABS), mortgage backed securities (MBS), and real estate investment trusts (REITs) ( Jung 2010). In this regard, Lee (2007: 22) concludes that the financial crisis influenced the Korean government to lay the foundation of real estate finance while discarding the public land concept.

The financial crisis and the resulting deregulation of real estate in the 1990s help explain why newspaper editorial writers became sensitive to tax issues, such as the landholding tax and the land transfer tax, and why those issues substituted for the public land concept in 1997. It thus seems likely that the financial crisis of 1997 influenced the land tenure system, from land owned by public entities to land becoming privately owned.

Another turning point was 2005. Given that the housing supply ratio reached 100 percent in 2002, the Roh Moo-Hyun administration turned its attention from housing provision to housing quality ( RonaldThe American Journal of Economics andSociology andJin 2010: 2373). What is more, it was understood that the rapid rise of real estate prices in the 2000s was due to rampant speculation rather than pure consumption demand for housing (Kim and Lee 2017;Ronald and Jin 2010). In 2005, the government deployed a real estate policy and measures to intervene in the real estate market and prevent speculation. The most important measure was the Comprehensive Real Estate Holding Tax. Other measures concerned housing welfare: subsidies for non-homeowners and a price ceiling on housing. Additional action involved housing supply, a transaction reporting system, and a process by which the public got restitution for land and housing redevelopment (Sung and Choi 2006).

This radical change in the real estate market met strong resistance from the public and the media (Kim and Lee 2017;Sung and Choi 2006). The proportion of "land-and-property topics" in editorials reached a 30-year peak in 2005, as shown in Figure 2. We have also observed that particular land topics (Seoul Metropolitan Area, greenbelt development, rent conflict, and speculation) were discussed less and less frequently in editorials in the following years (Figures 5,6,and 7). By contrast, topics concerning prices and redevelopment (reconstruction, redevelopment, and the price of renting or buying a house) received more attention. After analyzing the most frequently appearing topics ("house-rental price," "land-and-housing price," "redevelopment," and "reconstruction"), we saw that land underwent a change in meaning: land that was used as an object for urban development, especially for city planning and land development projects, increasingly became a commodity (La Grange and Jung 2004).

Pinpointing the cause of the downward trend in editorials on "rent conflict" and "land speculation" was difficult, based on the data we analyzed. In part, comprehensive real estate policy had an impact on rent conflict and on land speculation. Another possibility is that speculative practices had already become the norm by 2005, and editorial writers no longer saw a need to highlight this topic. Nonetheless, we found that speculation is still a crucial topic for public discussion in Korea. Recently, the Moon Jae-In administration planned to increase property taxes for multiple-home owners and short-term home sellers in order to reduce wealth inequality and property speculation (Choi Media and the Meanings of Land 2020;Kim and Lee 2017;Lee and Kim 2020). Therefore, even though we found that there has been a downward trend in references to "land speculation" from our editorial analysis, concerns about widespread "speculation" in Korean society remain. Clearly, further investigation of the issue of land speculation is needed.

In addition to unanswered questions about speculation, this research has two other limitations. First, due to the restrictions of accessibility to the online data, this research was able to obtain the data from only seven of the ten core newspapers. We suggest that further research must include the other three newspapers, all of which can be considered to be conservative. Second, the results point to the role of the media in constructing the culture of property, but the institutional reasons behind this role need further analysis. Also, it may be important to examine the property mind, which brings about the culture of property, from the perspectives of land buyers and developers in the Korean context.

paragraphs: The system of private property rights was introduced to Koreans at the beginning of Japanese colonization. In the 1970s, a real estate market emerged, and middle-class families have benefited from this system because the promotion of property rights provided them with opportunities to accumulate land and other assets. However, monetization of land caused various social problems in the 1970s and 1980s. After 1988, the Korean state, governed by civil leaders after decades of military rule, attempted to address those problems with land reforms. However, the IMF required that neoliberal policies be adopted as a condition for receipt of bailout funding in the wake of the 1997 Asian Financial Crisis. Those policies further deregulated land markets. In recent years, Korea has experienced unequal land development, with private ownership of land replacing public land. Land has become a commodity.

The meanings of land have changed throughout history and they may differ in different settings. In Singapore, Anne Haila (2017) questioned what motivates people to buy, sell, lease, and invest in the real estate market, leading to a "culture of property" and a shared "property

The American Journal of Economics and Sociology mind." The media tell stories that make investment in land common sense (Haila 2016). We attempted to explore changes in the meaning of land in Korea by examining newspaper editorials over the course of 30 years. We looked at how editorials have framed and reflected various meanings of land in response to changing socio-political and economic conditions. We also considered how partisanship (liberal vs. conservative) may have influenced which land-related topics were discussed in newspaper editorials. We found that liberal editorials tend to express more concern than conservatives do about social problems stemming from land and housing development and redevelopment.

We also discovered that socio-political conditions are key factors in shaping the treatment of land. The 1997 Asian Financial Crisis and the 2005 comprehensive real estate policy were crucial turning points in the political-economic history of land commodification in Korea. In 1997, Korean society experienced an unprecedented slump in the property market that triggered property deregulation. A long-term downward trend in discussion of the public land topic also began in 1997, while an upward trend emerged on topics related to taxation of land, which reveals an increase in private forms of land tenure. The rapid expansion of the property market in the early 2000s resulted in rising wealth inequality in Korea. This trend recalls Henry George's ([1879] 1981) arguments about poverty developing in tandem with progress. Indeed, in the case of Korea, inequality rose so steeply that desperate attempts were made to improve real estate policy, including the enactment of the Comprehensive Real Estate Holding Tax Act (2005).

Such policies were largely ineffective. Korea remains highly polarized (Koo 2019;Shin 2019). Based on our analysis, from 2005 onwards, editorials about the price of land and housing and about redevelopment and reconstruction overshadowed discussions about city planning and land development (including greenbelt development and the Seoul Metropolitan Area). Hence, we can conclude that "land being an object for urban development" has been replaced by "land being a commodity," as reflected in the editorials that we studied.

Korean media can be considered to be part and parcel of the culture of property. The media have been utilized as a communicative tool to promote particular forms of land tenure. The media have increasingly Media and the Meanings of Land emphasized private forms of land tenure and land commodification in their editorials since the 1990s. Finally, the media actively promote "the culture of property." Against the view that private property is natural or based solely on rational choice, we argue that "the property mind" is socially constructed and thus open for reconsideration. The media have played a key role in constructing the current understanding of land as a commodity. But since the media and their editorial content on land issues have been shaped by the history of land and housing development, the political situation, and social and economic conditions, there are still possibilities for developing new ways of seeing land and property.
sections.text:
The system of private property rights was introduced to Koreans at the beginning of Japanese colonization. In the 1970s, a real estate market emerged, and middle-class families have benefited from this system because the promotion of property rights provided them with opportunities to accumulate land and other assets. However, monetization of land caused various social problems in the 1970s and 1980s. After 1988, the Korean state, governed by civil leaders after decades of military rule, attempted to address those problems with land reforms. However, the IMF required that neoliberal policies be adopted as a condition for receipt of bailout funding in the wake of the 1997 Asian Financial Crisis. Those policies further deregulated land markets. In recent years, Korea has experienced unequal land development, with private ownership of land replacing public land. Land has become a commodity.

The meanings of land have changed throughout history and they may differ in different settings. In Singapore, Anne Haila (2017) questioned what motivates people to buy, sell, lease, and invest in the real estate market, leading to a "culture of property" and a shared "property

The American Journal of Economics and Sociology mind." The media tell stories that make investment in land common sense (Haila 2016). We attempted to explore changes in the meaning of land in Korea by examining newspaper editorials over the course of 30 years. We looked at how editorials have framed and reflected various meanings of land in response to changing socio-political and economic conditions. We also considered how partisanship (liberal vs. conservative) may have influenced which land-related topics were discussed in newspaper editorials. We found that liberal editorials tend to express more concern than conservatives do about social problems stemming from land and housing development and redevelopment.

We also discovered that socio-political conditions are key factors in shaping the treatment of land. The 1997 Asian Financial Crisis and the 2005 comprehensive real estate policy were crucial turning points in the political-economic history of land commodification in Korea. In 1997, Korean society experienced an unprecedented slump in the property market that triggered property deregulation. A long-term downward trend in discussion of the public land topic also began in 1997, while an upward trend emerged on topics related to taxation of land, which reveals an increase in private forms of land tenure. The rapid expansion of the property market in the early 2000s resulted in rising wealth inequality in Korea. This trend recalls Henry George's ([1879] 1981) arguments about poverty developing in tandem with progress. Indeed, in the case of Korea, inequality rose so steeply that desperate attempts were made to improve real estate policy, including the enactment of the Comprehensive Real Estate Holding Tax Act (2005).

Such policies were largely ineffective. Korea remains highly polarized (Koo 2019;Shin 2019). Based on our analysis, from 2005 onwards, editorials about the price of land and housing and about redevelopment and reconstruction overshadowed discussions about city planning and land development (including greenbelt development and the Seoul Metropolitan Area). Hence, we can conclude that "land being an object for urban development" has been replaced by "land being a commodity," as reflected in the editorials that we studied.

Korean media can be considered to be part and parcel of the culture of property. The media have been utilized as a communicative tool to promote particular forms of land tenure. The media have increasingly Media and the Meanings of Land emphasized private forms of land tenure and land commodification in their editorials since the 1990s. Finally, the media actively promote "the culture of property." Against the view that private property is natural or based solely on rational choice, we argue that "the property mind" is socially constructed and thus open for reconsideration. The media have played a key role in constructing the current understanding of land as a commodity. But since the media and their editorial content on land issues have been shaped by the history of land and housing development, the political situation, and social and economic conditions, there are still possibilities for developing new ways of seeing land and property.
